prompt: In ICD-10-CM, codes are generally reported to the highest level of specificity supported by documentation. What does that mean?

options.text: Use the most detailed valid code that matches the documented diagnosis
Always use three-character category codes only
Round all diagnoses to the nearest body system
Code symptoms instead of confirmed diagnoses whenever possible

explanation: ICD-10-CM guidelines require coding to the highest specificity supported by the record; truncating without reason can cause denials.

distractorExplanations.b: Three-character codes are used only when no further subdivision exists.
distractorExplanations.c: Body-system rounding is not a coding rule.
distractorExplanations.d: When a definitive diagnosis is documented, code it rather than symptoms alone.

prompt: A provider documents “rule out pneumonia” in an outpatient encounter. How should the coder generally proceed for outpatient ICD-10-CM?

options.text: Always assign the pneumonia code as primary
Code the signs/symptoms or confirmed conditions documented; do not code “rule out” as if confirmed
Assign Z00.00 for every rule-out visit
Omit all diagnosis codes until culture returns

explanation: Outpatient guidelines: do not code uncertain diagnoses as if established; code the documented symptoms/findings instead.

distractorExplanations.a: Coding unconfirmed conditions as established is incorrect outpatient practice.
distractorExplanations.c: Z00.00 is for general adult exams without abnormal findings, not every rule-out.
distractorExplanations.d: Claims still need reportable diagnoses based on available documentation.

prompt: Which CPT concept best describes a procedure that is always included in another procedure and generally not separately reportable?

options.text: Add-on code that must stand alone
Unlisted procedure requiring no documentation
Bundled / integral component of a more comprehensive service
Place-of-service modifier only

explanation: NCCI and CPT bundling concepts prevent separate payment for integral components already included in a primary service.

distractorExplanations.a: Add-on codes are reported with a primary code, not as standalone bundled services.
distractorExplanations.b: Unlisted codes still require documentation and often special reporting.
distractorExplanations.d: POS is not the bundling concept.

prompt: When selecting an E/M office visit level under current AMA CPT guidelines (MDM or time), the coder should primarily rely on:

options.text: Only the number of bullets historically used in 1995 exam checklists
The patient’s preference for a higher level
The charge master price alone
The provider’s documented medical decision making or total time on the date of the encounter

explanation: Office/outpatient E/M levels are selected by MDM or total time; outdated bullet counting is not the current standard.

distractorExplanations.a: 1995/1997 bullet systems are no longer the primary office E/M method.
distractorExplanations.b: Patient preference does not set CPT level.
distractorExplanations.c: Price is not a coding criterion.

prompt: A CPT modifier used to indicate a significant, separately identifiable E/M service on the same day as a procedure is:

options.text: Modifier 25
modifier 59
modifier 26
modifier 51

explanation: Modifier 25 is used when an E/M is significant and separately identifiable from the procedure on the same day.

distractorExplanations.b: 59 is for distinct procedural services, not E/M with procedure.
distractorExplanations.c: 26 indicates professional component.

prompt: Which statement about HCPCS Level II codes is correct?

options.text: They replace ICD-10-CM for all diagnoses
They commonly identify supplies, drugs, and certain services not in CPT
They are only used for inpatient DRG grouping
They are identical to CPT Category III codes

explanation: HCPCS Level II covers many non-CPT items (DME, drugs, some services) for billing.

distractorExplanations.c: DRGs use ICD diagnosis/procedure grouping, not HCPCS alone.
distractorExplanations.d: Category III CPT codes are temporary CPT tracking codes, not HCPCS II.

prompt: Sequencing of the principal/first-listed diagnosis should reflect:

options.text: Whichever code pays the highest amount
The alphabetically first diagnosis in the chart
The main reason for the encounter/admission as defined by official guidelines
Only chronic conditions, never acute problems

explanation: Official sequencing rules prioritize the condition chiefly responsible for the visit/admission, not payment maximization.

distractorExplanations.a: Coding for payment rather than guidelines is noncompliant.
distractorExplanations.b: Alphabetical order is not a sequencing rule.
distractorExplanations.d: Acute problems often drive sequencing when they are the reason for care.

prompt: Laterality in ICD-10-CM means the coder should:

options.text: Always default to bilateral for any paired organ
Ignore side because payers do not care
Use only unspecified laterality to avoid audits
Select right, left, bilateral, or unspecified codes when the classification provides them and documentation supports them

explanation: When laterality is available and documented, use the specific side; unspecified is used only when documentation lacks detail.

distractorExplanations.a: Bilateral is not a default without documentation.
distractorExplanations.b: Payers and guidelines expect accurate laterality.
distractorExplanations.c: Defaulting to unspecified when detail exists is incorrect.

prompt: A claim lists a CPT procedure without a diagnosis that supports medical necessity. The most likely result is:

options.text: Denial or request for additional documentation for lack of medical necessity link
Automatic upgrade to a higher paying code
Conversion of CPT to ICD-10-PCS by the payer
Immediate fraud conviction without review

explanation: Procedure–diagnosis linkage and medical necessity are core claim requirements; mismatches commonly cause denials.

distractorExplanations.b: Payers do not upgrade codes for missing necessity.
distractorExplanations.c: Code set conversion is not how necessity denials work.
distractorExplanations.d: Denial/review precedes any legal action; do not overstate.

prompt: Category II CPT codes are primarily used for:

options.text: Replacing all Evaluation and Management codes
Performance measurement / quality tracking, not typically for payment of the service itself
Reporting inpatient operating room procedures only
Identifying ambulance mileage exclusively

explanation: Category II codes support quality/performance reporting and are supplemental.

distractorExplanations.c: OR procedures use Category I / ICD-10-PCS as applicable.
distractorExplanations.d: Ambulance mileage uses HCPCS, not Category II broadly.

prompt: When a code description says “separate procedure,” it typically means:

options.text: It must always be billed twice
It can never be reported under any circumstance
It is usually not reported when performed with a related, more comprehensive procedure in the same area
It is only for Medicare Advantage plans

explanation: “Separate procedure” designation signals limited separate reporting when a larger related service is performed.

distractorExplanations.b: It may be reportable when truly independent/unrelated per rules.
distractorExplanations.d: The concept is CPT-wide, not plan-specific.

prompt: ICD-10-CM Excludes1 notes generally indicate:

options.text: The codes must always be billed as a pair
The note can be ignored if the charge is high
Only HCPCS is affected
The two conditions should not be coded together (mutually exclusive) when the note applies

explanation: Excludes1 means not coded together (with limited exceptions noted in guidelines); Excludes2 means not included here but both may be coded if present.

distractorExplanations.a: Excludes1 is not a pairing requirement.
distractorExplanations.b: Guidelines cannot be ignored for payment.
distractorExplanations.c: Excludes notes are ICD-10-CM concepts.

prompt: For CPT surgery coding, the global surgical package commonly includes:

options.text: The procedure and typical related preoperative/postoperative care within the global period, as defined by payer rules
All unrelated E/M visits forever
Only the anesthesia professional fee
Hospital room and board charges coded as CPT

explanation: Global periods bundle routine related pre/post care with the surgery; unrelated services may be separately reportable with modifiers when rules allow.

distractorExplanations.b: Unrelated care is not endlessly bundled.
distractorExplanations.c: Anesthesia is often billed separately by anesthesia providers.
distractorExplanations.d: Facility charges are not the same as CPT surgeon global package.

prompt: A coder finds conflicting documentation between a progress note and an operative report. Best practice is to:

options.text: Always pick the code that reimburses more
Query the provider for clarification before final coding when the conflict affects code selection
Delete the operative report from the record
Code both conflicting diagnoses as primary

explanation: Compliant coding requires resolving conflicts via query/clarification rather than guessing for revenue.

distractorExplanations.c: Altering records improperly is prohibited.
distractorExplanations.d: Two primaries from a conflict is not the fix.

prompt: Which is true about Z codes (ICD-10-CM factors influencing health status)?

options.text: They are never allowed on any claim
They replace CPT for procedures
They may be used as primary or secondary per guideline when they describe the reason for the encounter
They are only for dental claims

explanation: Z codes report encounters for circumstances other than disease/injury and can be first-listed when appropriate (e.g., aftercare, screening).

distractorExplanations.a: Z codes are widely used when guidelines support them.

prompt: On a professional CMS-1500 claim, the rendering provider’s NPI is typically reported to identify:

options.text: Who performed the service
The patient’s employer tax ID only
The ICD-10 guidelines version
The pharmacy NDC exclusively

explanation: Rendering NPI identifies the clinician who provided the service; billing NPI may differ in group settings.

distractorExplanations.b: Employer tax ID is not the rendering NPI purpose.
distractorExplanations.c: Guideline version is not an NPI field.
distractorExplanations.d: NDCs identify drugs, not rendering clinicians.

prompt: A clean claim is best described as:

options.text: Any claim submitted twice in one day
A complete, accurate claim that can be processed without needing additional information for basic adjudication
A claim missing diagnosis codes on purpose
A claim sent only by fax

explanation: Clean claims reduce delays; they include required data elements and pass initial edits.

distractorExplanations.c: Missing required data creates unclean claims.
distractorExplanations.d: Submission method alone does not define cleanliness.

prompt: Coordination of benefits (COB) is needed when:

options.text: The patient has no insurance
Only cash-pay encounters occur
A patient has more than one active payer and primary/secondary order must be determined
The provider changes office paint colors

explanation: COB establishes which plan pays first so secondary billing is correct.

prompt: An explanation of benefits (EOB) / electronic remittance advice primarily shows:

options.text: The provider’s personal credit score
Only the patient’s appointment preferences
A new CPT manual for the year
How the payer adjudicated the claim, including payment, adjustments, and patient responsibility

explanation: EOBs/ERAs communicate payer decisions and amounts; billers use them for posting and appeals.

distractorExplanations.b: Scheduling prefs are not remittance data.
distractorExplanations.c: Code books are separate publications.

prompt: A common reason for claim rejection at the clearinghouse level is:

options.text: Missing or invalid required data elements (e.g., NPI, DOB, diagnosis pointer)
The patient liking the physician
Correct ICD and CPT linkage with all fields complete
Timely filing within payer limits

explanation: Front-end edits catch format/validity errors before payer adjudication.

distractorExplanations.b: Patient satisfaction is not an edit.
distractorExplanations.c: Complete accurate claims should pass basic edits.
distractorExplanations.d: Timely filing is a payer denial reason later, not a typical clearinghouse format reject.

prompt: Timely filing limits refer to:

options.text: How long a patient may wait in the lobby
The deadline after the date of service by which a claim must be submitted to the payer
The shelf life of paper superbills only
The number of modifiers allowed

explanation: Missing timely filing often causes non-appealable denials; billers track payer-specific deadlines.

distractorExplanations.c: Not limited to paper superbills.
distractorExplanations.d: Modifier count is a coding/edit issue.

prompt: A deductible is:

options.text: The provider’s Medicare participation status
A type of CPT Category III code
The amount a patient must pay out of pocket before certain plan benefits pay
The clearinghouse enrollment fee only

explanation: Deductibles are patient cost-sharing features that affect balances after adjudication.

distractorExplanations.a: Participation is a provider-payer contract status.
distractorExplanations.d: Enrollment fees are administrative, not patient deductibles.

prompt: When a claim is denied as “not medically necessary,” the biller’s first compliance-oriented step is often to:

options.text: Change the diagnosis to whatever pays without documentation
Bill the patient for fraud penalties
Delete the encounter from the EHR
Review documentation, coding, and payer policy; correct if needed or appeal with clinical support

explanation: Appeals and corrections must be documentation-supported; fabricating codes is improper.

distractorExplanations.b: Patients are not billed “fraud penalties” as a routine AR step.
distractorExplanations.c: Deleting encounters improperly is prohibited.

prompt: Place of service (POS) codes on professional claims indicate:

options.text: Where the service was rendered (e.g., office, outpatient hospital, telehealth setting as applicable)
The patient’s home ZIP only for all claims
The ICD-10-CM seventh character
The anesthesia ASA physical status

explanation: POS affects pricing and coverage; it must match the actual setting.

distractorExplanations.b: ZIP is address data, not POS.
distractorExplanations.c: Seventh characters are diagnosis coding.

prompt: A National Drug Code (NDC) is most relevant when billing:

options.text: Routine office visit E/M without medications
Certain drugs/biologicals that payers require with HCPCS drug codes
ICD-10-CM Z-code screenings only
Ambulance mileage exclusively

explanation: Many payers require NDC detail for injectable/drug claims alongside HCPCS.

distractorExplanations.a: Pure E/M without drugs typically needs no NDC.
distractorExplanations.c: Z codes are diagnoses, not NDCs.

prompt: Which action best supports successful secondary payer billing?

options.text: Bill secondary first in every case
Ignore COB and bill both as primary duplicates
Submit the secondary claim with primary EOB/ERA data showing what primary paid and adjusted
Omit diagnosis codes on secondary claims

explanation: Secondaries need primary adjudication details to process remaining liability correctly.

distractorExplanations.a: Order is determined by COB rules.

prompt: An Advance Beneficiary Notice (ABN) in Medicare contexts is used when:

options.text: Every claim automatically needs an ABN
It replaces the CMS-1500 form
It is only for workers’ compensation
A provider expects Medicare may deny a service as not reasonable/necessary and must inform the beneficiary before furnishing it

explanation: ABNs shift financial liability appropriately when Medicare denial is expected for certain reasons; improper use can be invalid.

distractorExplanations.b: ABN is a notice, not a claim form.
distractorExplanations.c: It is a Medicare (and similar) concept, not WC-only.

prompt: Capitation payment models typically mean:

options.text: The plan pays a per-member periodic amount; providers manage care within that budget
Every CPT line is paid fee-for-service at full charge
Patients pay the entire billed amount always
ICD codes are no longer reported

explanation: Capitation is prospective PMPM-style payment; claims/encounter data may still be required for tracking.

distractorExplanations.c: Patients still have cost-shares per plan, not always full charges.

prompt: A frequency limit denial usually indicates:

options.text: The provider’s NPI checksum failed
The service exceeds how often the payer covers it in a time period
The patient never had insurance
The claim had no date of service

explanation: Payers set unit/frequency edits (e.g., preventive visits, certain therapies).

distractorExplanations.c: Uninsured is eligibility, not frequency.
distractorExplanations.d: Missing DOS is a separate reject.

prompt: Which statement about remittance remark/CARC codes is correct?

options.text: They replace the need for CPT codes on future claims
They are patient vital signs
They explain why payment differs from the charge (denial, CO, PR, etc.)
They are only printed on superbills

explanation: Claim adjustment reason codes communicate adjudication logic for posting and appeals.

distractorExplanations.d: They appear on remits, not as superbill content.

prompt: Under HIPAA, protected health information (PHI) includes:

options.text: Individually identifiable health information held or transmitted by a covered entity/business associate in any form
Only paper charts stored on-site
De-identified data with no identifiers remaining
Publicly posted clinic hours

explanation: PHI covers identifiable health info in electronic, paper, or oral forms when held by covered entities/BAs.

distractorExplanations.b: Electronic and oral PHI are also covered.
distractorExplanations.c: Properly de-identified data is not PHI.

prompt: The HIPAA minimum necessary standard generally requires that workforce members:

options.text: Share full charts with any caller who asks
Access/use/disclose only the PHI needed to perform their job duties
Post diagnoses on social media for education
Email PHI to personal accounts for convenience

explanation: Minimum necessary limits unnecessary PHI exposure; treatment-related access has specific nuances but curiosity viewing is prohibited.

distractorExplanations.a: Verify identity and authority before disclosures.
distractorExplanations.c: Social media PHI sharing is a breach risk.

prompt: A billing specialist notices a coworker accessing a celebrity patient’s record without a work need. The specialist should:

options.text: Ignore it if the coworker is a friend
Access the same record to compare notes
Report the incident per organizational privacy policy
Print the record for home review

explanation: Unauthorized access is a privacy incident; reporting protects patients and the organization.

prompt: Upcoding is best defined as:

options.text: Using the correct code that matches documentation
Adding a diagnosis pointer on a claim form
Verifying insurance eligibility
Reporting a higher-level or more complex service than documentation supports

explanation: Upcoding is a common fraud/abuse risk when levels or procedures are inflated.

distractorExplanations.c: Eligibility checks are appropriate.

prompt: Unbundling refers to:

options.text: Billing component codes separately when a comprehensive code includes them, contrary to rules
Combining unrelated patients onto one claim
Using one diagnosis for multiple encounters correctly
Encrypting email attachments

explanation: Unbundling to increase payment violates NCCI/coding rules when components are not separately reportable.

distractorExplanations.c: Diagnosis reuse across encounters can be legitimate.

prompt: The False Claims Act risk is heightened when a practice:

options.text: Appeals a denial with supporting records
Knowingly submits claims that are false or fraudulent for payment
Documents medical necessity thoroughly
Uses certified EHR audit logs

explanation: Knowingly false claims (including reckless disregard) create FCA exposure; good-faith appeals differ.

distractorExplanations.c: Strong documentation supports compliance.

prompt: A valid authorization for non-routine PHI disclosure generally must include:

options.text: Only the patient’s nickname
A blank form with no purpose stated
A description of information, purpose, who may disclose/receive, expiration, and signature
The coder’s personal cell number as the recipient

explanation: HIPAA authorization core elements ensure the patient understands and limits the disclosure.

prompt: Which is an example of a potential HIPAA breach requiring assessment?

options.text: A nurse discusses a patient in a private treatment room with the care team
Filing a claim to the patient’s health plan for treatment payment
Using a fax cover sheet to a known provider number for continuity of care
A laptop with unencrypted ePHI is stolen from a car

explanation: Lost/stolen unencrypted devices with ePHI commonly trigger breach risk assessment and possible notification.

distractorExplanations.a: Treatment communications among the team are permitted uses.
distractorExplanations.b: Payment to health plans is a permitted purpose.
distractorExplanations.c: Appropriate care coordination disclosures can be allowed.

prompt: Stark Law (physician self-referral) generally concerns:

options.text: Physician referrals for certain designated health services to entities with which they have financial relationships, subject to exceptions
Only overtime pay for billers
FDA drug manufacturing
State board cosmetology licenses

explanation: Stark is a strict-liability self-referral statute for DHS; billing/coding staff should escalate suspect arrangements.

distractorExplanations.c: FDA is separate.

prompt: Anti-Kickback Statute concerns typically involve:

options.text: Normal salary for employed clinicians within fair market value without referral inducement analysis needs never
Offering or receiving remuneration to induce referrals for federal healthcare program business
Patient copay collection as required by plan
Publishing a clinic’s phone number

explanation: AKS is intent-based; improper gifts, sham consulting, or volume-based incentives tied to referrals are high risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Bona fide employment can fit safe harbors when structured properly—still not “never analyze.”
distractorExplanations.c: Collecting allowed cost-shares is normal.

prompt: Business associates under HIPAA are entities that:

options.text: Are always the patient’s family members
Never touch PHI by definition
Perform functions involving PHI for a covered entity and generally need a BAA
Only include the IRS

explanation: Billing companies, clearinghouses, and many vendors are BAs and require agreements and safeguards.

distractorExplanations.a: Family members are not BAs by default.
distractorExplanations.d: IRS is not the BA definition.

prompt: Waiving copays routinely for all insured patients to gain business may create compliance risk because:

options.text: Copays are illegal in the United States
HIPAA forbids collecting any money
CPT guidelines ban patient responsibility
It can implicate inducement/beneficiary incentive and payer contract/fraud concerns

explanation: Routine waiver of cost-sharing can be problematic; financial hardship policies should be documented and applied carefully.

distractorExplanations.c: CPT does not eliminate patient responsibility.

prompt: The best practice when emailing claim details containing PHI is to:

options.text: Use approved secure methods per policy (encryption/portal) and verify recipients
Send from a personal Gmail to any address
CC public listservs for faster payment
Include full SSN in the subject line

explanation: Security Rule expectations and policies require reasonable safeguards for ePHI in transit.

distractorExplanations.c: Public CCs are a breach.
distractorExplanations.d: SSNs in subjects are high risk.

prompt: Qui tam actions related to healthcare fraud allow:

options.text: Patients to prescribe controlled substances
Private persons (whistleblowers) to bring certain False Claims Act cases on behalf of the government
Coders to change medical records freely
Payers to skip remittance advice forever

explanation: Whistleblower provisions are a major FCA enforcement pathway; compliance programs encourage internal reporting first.

distractorExplanations.c: Record falsification is prohibited.

prompt: When a patient requests an accounting of disclosures, the covered entity generally must:

options.text: Refuse all patient rights requests automatically
Only give accounting to marketers
Provide an accounting of certain disclosures as required by the Privacy Rule, with allowed exceptions
Post the accounting on Facebook

explanation: Patients have rights to access, amendments (with process), and accounting of certain disclosures.

distractorExplanations.a: Patient rights must be honored per HIPAA.
distractorExplanations.b: Accounting is for the individual, not marketers.
distractorExplanations.d: Public posting would be a violation.

prompt: Front-end revenue cycle activities typically include:

options.text: Scheduling, registration, insurance verification, and collecting accurate demographics
Only writing off bad debt after years
Appeals after final denial exclusively
Coding the operative report before the patient arrives for a new problem

explanation: Clean data at registration prevents many denials downstream.

distractorExplanations.c: Appeals are mid/back-end.
distractorExplanations.d: You cannot code future undocumented surgeries.

prompt: Eligibility verification before a visit helps to:

options.text: Guarantee the claim will never deny for any reason
Confirm active coverage, plan rules, and often referral/authorization needs
Replace the need for diagnosis coding
Determine the patient’s blood type

explanation: Eligibility reduces surprises but does not eliminate all denial risks (medical necessity, coding, etc.).

distractorExplanations.c: Diagnoses remain required.

prompt: A referral (in managed care) is typically:

options.text: The patient’s thank-you card
A CPT modifier for bilateral procedures
Authorization from a PCP/plan allowing the patient to see a specialist or obtain certain services
The hospital’s charge description master

explanation: Missing referrals/authorizations are common preventable denials in HMO-style plans.

prompt: Charge capture means:

options.text: Deleting charges to hide productivity
Only collecting copays in cash
Turning off the practice management system
Recording all billable services/supplies provided so they can be coded and billed

explanation: Missed charges lose revenue; phantom charges create compliance risk—accuracy matters both ways.

distractorExplanations.a: Deleting legitimate charges improperly harms revenue integrity.
distractorExplanations.b: Copays are only one collection step.
distractorExplanations.c: Systems must remain operational for RCM.

prompt: Days in accounts receivable (A/R) measures:

options.text: How long, on average, claims/balances remain unpaid
Patient satisfaction stars only
The number of exam rooms
Vaccine refrigerator temperature

explanation: A/R days is a core RCM KPI; high days may signal denial, follow-up, or posting problems.

distractorExplanations.c: Room count is operations.

prompt: Patient estimates / good-faith estimates support:

options.text: Guaranteed final bills with zero variance always
Transparency about expected out-of-pocket costs before or at service
Elimination of EOBs
Skipping insurance filing forever

explanation: Estimates improve collections and compliance with price transparency expectations; final adjudication may differ.

distractorExplanations.a: Estimates can change with clinical reality.
distractorExplanations.c: EOBs still issue after claims.

prompt: A denial management workflow should prioritize:

options.text: Resubmitting the identical unclean claim daily forever without review
Writing off every denial immediately without analysis
Root-cause analysis, correction, timely appeal/rebill, and prevention at the front end
Ignoring payer policies

explanation: Effective denial management fixes causes and meets appeal deadlines.

prompt: Credit balances in patient accounts should be:

options.text: Kept indefinitely as clinic profit without review
Moved to the physician’s personal account
Ignored if under $10,000 without policy
Investigated and refunded or corrected per payer/patient rules and law

explanation: Credit balance compliance (including Medicare) requires timely resolution; retaining overpayments is risky.

distractorExplanations.c: Thresholds must follow law/policy—not casual ignore.

prompt: Which front-desk practice improves point-of-service collections?

options.text: Verifying benefits and collecting known copays/outstanding balances per policy before or at visit
Never discussing money with patients
Promising that insurance always pays 100%
Collecting a random amount unrelated to benefits

explanation: Clear communication and accurate benefit knowledge improve POS collections without misrepresentation.

distractorExplanations.b: Financial discussions are appropriate and expected.
distractorExplanations.c: Guaranteeing 100% payment is often false.

prompt: A charge description master (CDM) / fee schedule alignment issue can cause:

options.text: Improved ICD-10 laterality automatically
Wrong CPT/HCPCS mapped to charges, leading to billing errors or compliance risk
HIPAA authorization signatures
Vaccine potency increases

explanation: CDM maintenance is critical so charges map to correct codes and prices.

distractorExplanations.c: Authorizations are privacy forms.

prompt: Prior authorization failure most often results in:

options.text: Automatic increase in E/M level
Conversion to inpatient DRG for all office visits
Denial of payment for the service when the plan required auth and it was not obtained
Deletion of the patient’s demographics

explanation: Many high-cost services need auth; staff should verify before scheduling/performing when required.

distractorExplanations.a: Auth issues do not raise E/M.

prompt: Encounter forms / superbills are most useful when they:

options.text: List only the highest paying codes regardless of services
Omit diagnoses to save time
Are never reviewed after CPT updates
Reflect commonly used accurate diagnoses and procedures and are updated with coding changes

explanation: Outdated superbills drive wrong coding; they are tools, not substitutes for documentation.

distractorExplanations.a: Listing codes for inducement is abusive.
distractorExplanations.c: Annual code updates must be maintained.

prompt: Soft denials differ from hard denials in that soft denials often:

options.text: Can be corrected and resubmitted (e.g., missing info) without a formal appeal
Can never be fixed
Always indicate criminal fraud
Only apply to dental claims

explanation: Understanding denial type guides whether to rebill, appeal, or write off.

distractorExplanations.c: Most denials are administrative/clinical coverage—not automatic fraud.

prompt: Patient statements should generally:

options.text: Include other patients’ names for comparison
Clearly show balances, insurance pending vs patient responsibility, and payment options
Hide the dates of service
List staff Social Security numbers

explanation: Clear statements aid collections and reduce call volume; privacy must be maintained.

distractorExplanations.a: Other patients’ PHI is a violation.
distractorExplanations.c: DOS helps patients recognize services.

prompt: Revenue cycle analytics that track top denial reasons help organizations to:

options.text: Eliminate the need for coding staff
Stop verifying eligibility
Target training, payer contract issues, and process fixes where losses concentrate
Ignore medical necessity documentation

explanation: Data-driven denial prevention is a hallmark of mature RCM programs.
